summary: Gruber's comprehensive review of the iPhone 4 covers its refined hardware design, the stunning Retina Display, improved performance via the A4 chip, FaceTime video calling, and the subtle switch from Helvetica to Helvetica Neue as the system font. He praises the build quality as approaching an idealized iPhone form factor, with flat sides, better buttons, and a smaller yet more capable body. He addresses the two widely-reported problems — the antenna/reception issue and the proximity sensor — with measured analysis of possible outcomes. The review positions the iPhone 4 not as a reinvention but as a dramatic clarification of the original iPhone's vision.
key_points: The Retina Display produces print-quality rendering that transforms the experience, with LCD pixels closer to the glass surface than any previous device
FaceTime is predicted to become one of Apple's most important introductions, functioning as a de facto social network that drives device purchases
Apple switched the system font from Helvetica to Helvetica Neue specifically for the Retina Display, reflecting their obsession with hardware-software integration details
The antenna reception issue is multivariate — varying by location, individual unit, and hand placement — making it unclear whether software alone can fix it
The iPhone 4's camera quality threatens dedicated devices like the Flip HD, especially combined with on-device editing and connectivity
money_quotes: It hasn't really changed so much as improved — like the same picture in increasingly sharper focus.
No other company gives a shit about things like this.
The overall build quality seems impossibly good. The iPhone 4 is beautiful to behold and feels like a valuable artifact. It's like a love letter to Dieter Rams.
With the iPhone 4, it's like dropping a piece of toast that's been buttered on both sides.
Normal people aren't just going to use FaceTime — they're going to love it.
Mark these words: FaceTime goes down as one of the most important things Apple has ever introduced.
It is a more Helvetica-y Helvetica.
That Apple pays so much attention to the details as to pick a different version of Helvetica for different classes of displays is emblematic of what makes the iPhone the iPhone.
key_insight: The iPhone 4 represents not reinvention but relentless refinement — the same fundamental vision as the original iPhone, brought into sharper focus through obsessive attention to hardware-software integration details that no other company bothers with.
